title: Product Strategy & New Product Development
simple_html:
A product is anything a company offers to satisfy a need or want — and it is far more than a physical object. Marketers think of a product in three layers: the core benefit (the real value the customer is buying), the actual product (features, design, brand name, packaging), and the augmented product (delivery, warranty, support, after-sales service). When you buy a drill, the core benefit is really a hole in the wall.

Why it matters

Companies live or die by their products. Most products follow a Product Life Cycle (PLC): Introduction (slow sales, high cost), Growth (sales accelerate, competitors enter), Maturity (sales peak, price competition), and Decline (sales fall). Each stage needs a different marketing approach.

Building new products

Because every product eventually ages, firms must keep developing new ones. The classic New Product Development (NPD) funnel moves ideas through stages: idea generation, screening, concept testing, business analysis, product development, test marketing, and commercialization. Most ideas are killed early — that is the point.

Key takeaways

  • Sell the benefit, not just the features — customers buy solutions.
  • Match your marketing to the PLC stage: build awareness early, defend share at maturity.
  • NPD is a filtering funnel: many ideas in, few products out.
  • New products are risky — most fail — so screen ruthlessly and test before launch.
  • A strong product line and mix (width, length, depth) spreads risk and serves more segments.
intermediate_html:
Product strategy decides what a firm sells, how its offerings are structured, and how it renews them over time. It is the "Product" pillar of the marketing mix and the engine of long-run growth.

The three product levels

Kotler's model defines a product in layers: the core benefit (the fundamental need solved), the actual product (features, quality, design, brand, packaging), and the augmented product (warranty, installation, service, financing). Differentiation increasingly happens at the augmented level, where competitors find it harder to copy.

Product hierarchy and mix

Firms manage portfolios using product-mix dimensions: width (number of product lines), length (total items), depth (variants per item), and consistency (how related the lines are). Procter & Gamble runs a wide, deep mix; a boutique runs a narrow one.

The Product Life Cycle

The PLC maps sales and profit across four stages and prescribes distinct tactics:

StageGoalTactics
IntroductionCreate awareness, trialHeavy promotion, skim or penetrate pricing
GrowthMaximize shareExtend features, broaden distribution
MaturityDefend share, harvest profitDifferentiate, promote, cut costs
DeclineHarvest or exitReduce spend, milk or divest

The NPD process

The standard stage-gate funnel: (1) Idea generation (internal R&D, customers, competitors), (2) Screening (kill weak ideas cheaply), (3) Concept development & testing (turn an idea into a tangible concept and test with target customers), (4) Marketing strategy & business analysis (forecast sales, costs, profit), (5) Product development (build a prototype), (6) Test marketing (limited real-world launch), (7) Commercialization (full launch). Gates between stages allow go/kill decisions before big money is spent.

Worked mini-case

A snack company has 100 raw ideas. Screening kills 70. Concept testing rejects 20 more. Business analysis drops 7. Of the 3 prototypes, 1 survives test marketing and launches. This roughly 100-to-1 attrition is normal and explains why disciplined screening matters: it concentrates spend on winners.

How to apply it

  • Audit each product's PLC stage and align budget — do not over-invest in declining lines.
  • Use stage-gates with explicit kill criteria so emotion does not keep dead ideas alive.
  • Look for differentiation in the augmented layer (service, warranty) where margins and loyalty live.
  • Balance the mix: cash from mature "cash cows" funds the next generation of products.
full_html:
Product strategy is the discipline of deciding what a firm offers, how those offerings are architected into lines and mixes, and how the portfolio is continuously renewed through innovation. Because the product is the value the customer ultimately experiences, it anchors the entire marketing mix (see Marketing Mix (4Ps), Lesson 1) and is the principal driver of sustainable growth.

Defining the product: levels of value

Kotler & Keller (2016) describe five product levels that nest outward: the core benefit (the fundamental service the customer buys), the basic product, the expected product (attributes buyers normally assume), the augmented product (offerings that exceed expectations — warranty, service, financing), and the potential product (all future augmentations). Theodore Levitt's classic dictum captures the core-benefit idea: customers do not want a quarter-inch drill, they want a quarter-inch hole (Levitt, 1960; 1980). Levitt (1980) further argued that even commodities can be differentiated at the augmented and expected levels — the basis of much modern service competition.

Product classifications

Consumer products split into convenience (frequent, low-effort), shopping (compared on quality/price), specialty (unique, brand-loyal), and unsought goods (insurance, pre-need). Industrial products split into materials and parts, capital items, and supplies/services — a distinction that links to business buyer behavior (Lesson 3) and B2B selling.

Product-mix architecture

A firm's offering is structured along four dimensions (Kotler & Keller, 2016): width (number of distinct product lines), length (total number of items across lines), depth (variants — sizes, flavors, formulations — per item), and consistency (how closely related the lines are in use, production, or channel). Line decisions include line stretching (adding higher- or lower-end items), line filling (adding items within the current range), and line pruning. Portfolio choices connect directly to the BCG growth-share matrix and the Ansoff matrix (Lesson 4 strategic tools): cash cows fund question marks and new-product bets.

The Product Life Cycle (PLC)

The PLC (Levitt, 1965) posits that products move through Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline, each with characteristic sales, profit, cost, and competitive intensity, and therefore distinct strategic imperatives. In Introduction, the priority is awareness and trial; pricing follows either a skimming strategy (high price, recover R&D from early adopters) or a penetration strategy (low price, build share fast). In Growth, the firm broadens distribution, adds features, and may cut price to deter entrants. In Maturity — typically the longest stage — competition is fiercest and growth comes from modifying the market, the product, or the marketing mix. In Decline, the firm harvests (minimize spend, milk cash flow) or divests.

Nuances and critiques. The PLC is descriptive, not deterministic: shape and duration vary widely (fads spike and crash; staples plateau for decades), and the model risks self-fulfilling prophecy — pulling support from a "mature" brand can manufacture its decline (Dhalla & Yuspeh, 1976). It is best used as a diagnostic heuristic, not a forecast.

New Product Development (NPD)

Booz, Allen & Hamilton (1982) codified the canonical NPD sequence, later formalized as stage-gate by Cooper (1990): (1) idea generation, (2) screening, (3) concept development and testing, (4) marketing strategy and business analysis, (5) product development, (6) test marketing, (7) commercialization. Each gate is a go/kill checkpoint with explicit criteria, so resources concentrate on survivors. The defining feature is steep attrition — historically many ideas are needed per commercial success — which makes cheap early screening the highest-leverage activity.

Diffusion of innovations. Rogers (1962) explains how new products spread through adopter categories — innovators (~2.5%), early adopters (~13.5%), early majority, late majority, and laggards — separated by Moore's (1991) "chasm" between early adopters and the pragmatic early majority. Crossing the chasm is where many technically successful products commercially fail.

Worked example: NPD funnel economics

Suppose a firm screens 64 ideas. If each gate passes 50%, the survivors per stage are 64 to 32 to 16 to 8 to 4 to 2 to 1 launched product. If total NPD spend is concentrated so that 80% is spent only after the business-analysis gate (8 survivors), then early-stage screening of 56 rejected ideas consumed only 20% of budget — illustrating why disciplined early kills protect the budget for the eventual winner. The lesson: the cost of a kill rises sharply at each later gate, so be ruthless early and rigorous late.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Marketing myopia — defining the product by its physical form rather than the customer benefit, leaving the firm blind to substitutes (Levitt, 1960).
  • Treating the PLC as a forecast rather than a heuristic, then starving brands into self-fulfilling decline.
  • Skipping gates — falling in love with a pet idea and bypassing screening or test marketing, the leading cause of costly launch failures.
  • Over-stretching the line — proliferating SKUs that cannibalize each other and inflate complexity costs.
  • Ignoring the chasm — assuming early-adopter enthusiasm predicts mainstream demand.

Cross-links

Product strategy feeds Pricing Strategy (Lesson 7, where skim/penetrate decisions live), Branding & Brand Equity (Lesson 5, the brand layer of the product), and Marketing Channels (Lesson 8, how the product reaches the buyer). Portfolio decisions draw on the BCG and Ansoff tools introduced in Lesson 4.
